title: Piston control in a two-ion quantum device
authors: Jing Li, E. Ya. Sherman, Andreas Ruschhaupt
abstract: We propose a scheme for piston control in a two-ion quantum device with motion confined to orthogonal axes. In this system, one ion plays the role of a ''classical'' piston driven by the Coulomb interaction with the other ion, whose quantum motion is controlled through modulation of its trapping potential. The stationary state is determined self-consistently, taking quantum effects into account. We identify a narrow quantum regime of the ground state connecting two broad classical regimes. We further design inverse-engineering protocols to control the motion of the ''classical'' ion. The proposed control scheme provides a useful route toward controlled piston dynamics in microscopic quantum devices.
summary: This paper details a method to control the motion of a heavy ion (piston) coupled to a light ion (quantum medium) in a two-ion quantum device. By using an inverse-engineering approach, the required time-dependent trapping potential for the light ion is calculated to achieve precise piston trajectories. The results confirm that this control scheme works robustly across classical, hybrid, and full quantum theoretical treatments.
main_problem: The paper proposes and analyzes a scheme for achieving controlled 'piston dynamics' in a two-ion quantum device.
main_result: An inverse-engineered control protocol for modulating the trapping potential of one ion successfully controls the motion of the other ion, showing high fidelity across multiple theoretical approximations.
method: The study employs a hybrid quantum-classical approach, treating the heavy ion classically while solving the Schrödinger equation for the light ion, and uses inverse engineering to design the required time-dependent control fields.
model_or_system: The system consists of two interacting ions confined in orthogonal harmonic traps, where the interaction is governed by the Coulomb potential. The lighter ion acts as a quantum working medium, and the heavier ion acts as a classical piston.
key_observables: Final-state fidelity (F), final excitation energy (Q), and the time-dependent mean position of the piston ($\langle \hat{X} angle$).
important_parameters: The time-dependent trapping frequency ($\omega_L(t)$) of the light ion, and the ratio of ion masses ($M/m$).
main_assumptions: The heavy ion is treated classically due to its large mass, and the analysis relies on hybrid quantum-classical approximations which are shown to agree well with the full quantum treatment.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the schematic setup, the comparison of stationary state regimes (single vs. double minimum), and crucially, compare the final excitation energy (Q) and fidelity (F) achieved by the inverse-engineered control versus an adiabatic reference protocol.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the Hamiltonian and the hybrid quantum-classical framework. It then analyzes the stationary states by identifying distinct quantum regimes based on trap frequencies. Finally, it focuses on time-dependent control, using inverse engineering to design the control pulse $\omega_L(t)$ and comparing its performance against other protocols.
why_interesting: This work provides a clean, controllable, and experimentally relevant model for studying quantum work extraction and controlled coupling dynamics using trapped ions, which is a cornerstone of modern AMO physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of the two-ion system in the x ⊗y geometry. The left ion is confined along the y direction and treated quantum me- chanically with the wavefunction ψ(y) corresponding to the effective potential V[eff] L . The right ion can move along the x axis in the po- tential VR(X) having the minimum at X = d and acts as an effective classical piston. Panels (a) and (b) illustrate the effective left-ion po- tential in the double-minimum and single-minimum realization, re- spectively. Details of the potential and corresponding notations will be presented below in the text.

FIG. 2. Stationary state, full quantum treatment: (a) right ion prob- ability densities ρR(X), (b) zoomed-in and shifted probability densi- ties ρR, and (c) the corresponding left ion probability densities ρL(y) for the values of ωL shown in the plots, see also Eq. (31). Here ωRtu = 20, d/lu = 10.

FIG. 4. Stationary state: Zoom in the transition region between the double-minimum and single-minimum regimes of Fig. 3 (b); full quantum treatment (joined filled marks), hybrid approxima- tion (joined open marks), classical approximation (solid line). In- set: probability density of the left ion in the full quantum calcu- lation ρL(y) (solid) and the hybrid approximation ψ(y) (dashed) for ωLtu = 75.22 (blue solid/magenta dashed) and ωLtu = 75.25 (red solid/black dashed).

FIG. 3. Stationary state: (a) Comparisons of positions of right ion among full quantum treatment ⟨ˆX⟩(filled markers), hybrid ap- proximation χ (open markers) and classical approximation X0 (solid lines) results are shown. (b) Corresponding positions of the left ion in the same three cases: aq, ah and a0 parameters as a function of ωL. Three parameter cases in both figures: ωRtu = 20, d/lu = 10 (green), ωRtu = 20, d/lu = 8 (black), and ωRtu = 25, d/lu = 10 (red).

FIG. 5. Control schemes corresponding to X0/lu = 20 and Xf /lu = 30, dR/lu = 10, ωRtu = 20. (a) Designed position of left particle a(t) for different final times t f . (inset) Designed position of right particle X(t). (b) Designed control schemes ωL(t) for different final times t f as well as the adiabatic reference scheme (dashed line).
